segments.s685-g169: بگفت is a past narrative verb meaning “he said.” این here refers back to the (just) said thing — “this.” و is the coordinating conjunction linking the saying to the next action. از پیش is a short spatial phrase “from before / out of the presence of,” and آزادگان is a plural noun “the free ones / nobles”; together از پیش آزادگان means “from before the nobles.” Word order is terser/poetic: verb (بگفت) + object/pronoun (این) + conjunction + prepositional phrase (از پیش آزادگان).
segments.s685-g170: بیامد is a simple past verb “came.” سوی is a directional preposition/adverb “toward / to.” گلشن is a noun “garden,” and شادگان here qualifies the place (or its people) as “joyful” — a poetic name: “the garden of the joyful.” The clause follows the common Persian pattern verb + direction phrase (بیامد سوی ...).
segments.s685-g171: پراگنده گشت is a compound predicate: پراگنده (scattered, dispersed) + گشت (became) — together “became scattered” or “was dispersed.” آن بزرگ انجمن is a noun phrase “that great assembly.” Note the somewhat inverted, poetic word order: predicate before the full noun phrase it describes.
segments.s685-g172: همه is the distributive pronoun “all / everyone.” رخ means “face.” پر is an adjectival element meaning “full (of)” and attaches directly before a following noun to mean “full of X.” آژنگ here is a poetic noun referring to facial color/ornamentation (make‑up or blush) — so رخ پر آژنگ = “faces full of make‑up/blush.” و = “and.” دل is “heart,” and پرشکن is a compound adjective meaning literally “full‑breaking” → “heart‑breaking” or “hearts broken.” The phrase therefore describes everyone’s faces and hearts at once: “all faces were full of makeup and hearts were broken.”
segments.s685-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' (not simile); خورشید is the subject 'the sun'. خنجر کشید is a past simple verbal phrase 'drew (a) dagger' where کشید is the past of کشیدن; از نیام is a prepositional phrase 'from the scabbard' with از marking origin. Word order is subject + verb + complement (typical Persian inversion for poetic effect).
segments.s685-g2: پدید آمد is a compound verbal phrase meaning 'appeared/ emerged' (پدید + آمد). آن is the demonstrative 'that/the' pointing to a person, here introducing the name/epithet مطرف; زردفام is an adjectival epithet 'yellow‑faced/yellow‑hued' following the noun. The whole unit means 'that Motarraf, yellow‑complexioned, appeared.'
segments.s685-g3: فرستاد is the past 'he sent' (here purposeful sending/dispatch). و links actions 'and'. گردنکشان را is the definite object 'the rebels' marked by را. بخواند is a past (poetic) form meaning 'he called/summoned' (not 'read'); together the clause says he sent for and summoned the rebels.
segments.s685-g4: برتخت شاهی is a prepositional phrase 'on the royal throne' (برتخت = 'on the throne', شاهی qualifying it). به زانو is a locative expression literally 'to/on the knee' (i.e. on his knee/lap). نشاند is an archaic past-form of 'to seat' (he seated/placed them). The verb completes the action on the earlier object (گردنکشان را).
segments.s685-g5: بهرجای is 'to each place / in every place' (distributive), کرسی زرین is 'golden seat/throne' (noun + adjective), and نهاد is the past of نهادن 'he placed/put'. The clause thus states that golden thrones were set everywhere.
segments.s685-g6: چوشاهان is a fused poetic phrase meaning 'when the kings' (چو = 'when'), پیروز is an adjective 'victorious', بنشست is past 'sat down', and شاد is 'happy'. The clause gives the result/state when the victorious kings took their seats.
segments.s685-g7: چنین is 'thus/so'. گفت here functions as the reported-speech verb 'said' (the lexical senses listed in the data are conversational nouns, so the precise verbal sense is the usual 'he said' in context). زان پس = 'after that/thereupon' (زان = 'from that'; پس = 'after/then'). به بانگ بلند is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'with a loud voice' (به + بانگ, بلند qualifies the sound).
segments.s685-g8: که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the reported content. هرکس که هست is a relative clause 'whoever is' (هرکس = whoever; the second که + هست = 'who is'); ازشما = 'of you / among you' (possessive/partitive), and ارجمند is a predicate adjective 'esteemed/honored'. The clause reads 'that whoever among you is honored...' and continues in the next line.
segments.s685-g145: کنون ('now') is a temporal adverb placed at the start. تاکسی is an archaic/colloquial contraction of تا + کسی meaning 'if/until someone' and introduces the indefinite subject; از + نژادکیان ('from the race/lineage of the Kiyan') marks origin/lineage with از followed by the genitive plural noun.
segments.s685-g146: بیاید is subjunctive/jussive ('may come' / 'should come') governing the following purpose/sequence. ببندد کمر is an idiomatic clause 'to bind/gird the loins' (ببندد is present-subjunctive of بستن); کمر here means the belt/waist (girdle). بر میان ('about the middle') uses the preposition بر + میان to indicate around/at the middle.
segments.s685-g147: هم آن به که is an idiomatic concessive/optative phrase meaning 'it is likewise/also fitting that...' followed by a clause introduced by که. این برنشیند is subjunctive/optative ('that this one sit / be seated'); بتخت is a poetic/contracted form corresponding to 'بر تخت' ('on the throne'), here functioning as the locative complement.
segments.s685-g148: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('who/for he is'). گردست here names a physical/strength quality (literally the wrist/arm area) used as a martial attribute; و links a series of attributes: جنگاور ('warrior' / 'warlike') and نیک‌بخت ('fortunate'), both predicative describing the same person.
segments.s685-g149: سرجنگیان is a title/noun ('the war-leader/commander') serving as the subject. کاین سخنها شنید is a past-tense clause: کاین ('these/that') + سخنها ('words/speeches') as object and شنید (past of شنیدن) 'he heard', placing the event in the past.
segments.s685-g150: This is a sequence of two past actions: بزد دست ('he struck with his hand') is an idiomatic transitive clause where بزد (past of زدن) takes دست as the instrumental/body-part complement; و joins the next action تیغ از میان برکشید, where تیغ ('sword') is the object, از میان ('from between') gives the source, and برکشید (past of برکشیدن) is a literary verb meaning 'to draw/lift' (here 'to draw a sword').
segments.s685-g151: چنین گفت is a conventional formula 'he said thus' (چنین 'thus' + گفت 'said'); کز تخم شاهان زنی is a relative/partitive phrase meaning 'that/from the seed of kings a woman' where کز (contraction of که + از) marks origin, تخم ('seed, origin') + شاهان ('of kings') gives pedigree, and زنی ('a woman') is the noun being spoken of.
segments.s685-g152: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. باز yābim ('again') here combines with یابیم (یابید#یاب) in the subjunctive/optative form یابیم — grammatically marking 'if we may find / if we should find'. در بر زنی is a small prepositional phrase 'into the bosom/embrace a woman' (در + بر used idiomatically for 'in/into the arms'), with زنی the object.
segments.s685-g129: چو introduces a temporal clause here meaning "when." ناپاک‌دل is a compound adjective: ناپاک modifies دل (heart) to mean "impure‑hearted" (morally corrupt). خوشنواز is an epithet applied to a person (literally "pleasant‑player") and here functions as a substantive/epithet for a musician/minstrel.
segments.s685-g130: که introduces a relative clause "who/that." گم کرد is a past tense verbal phrase meaning "lost" (گم + کرد, where کرد is the simple past auxiliary/verb). زین is poetic/contracted for زِ این ("from this") rather than the literal noun "saddle"; بوم و بر is an idiomatic pair meaning "land and home/territory," and نام و ناز means "name (fame) and nâz (favor/pride)" — so the clause says he lost his fame and favour in/from this land.
segments.s685-g131: چو = "when." پیروز is used as an adjective "victorious." شاهی here qualifies the royal character ("kingly/royal"), followed by بلند اختری, a poetic adjectival phrase "lofty, star‑like," where بلند = "lofty" and اختری = "star‑like/stellar." The sequence stacks epithets before the following noun.
segments.s685-g132: جهاندار is a title meaning "world‑ruler/sovereign." وز here is a contracted poetic form (likely و ز = «و از» or similar) used to link and compare and should not be read as the literal noun "gust/weight"; نامداران is the plural adjective/noun "the renowned/illustrious," and سری means "head/chief." The phrase thus reads "the sovereign, and chief among the renowned."
segments.s685-g133: بکشتند is the simple past, third‑person plural "they killed." هیتالیان is a proper noun (the Hitalians) as the agent, and ناگهان is an adverb "suddenly," modifying the action.
segments.s685-g134: نگون شد is a two‑word verbal phrase meaning "was overthrown/was made to fall" (نگون = overthrown/downfallen; شد = became/was). سرتخت here refers to the royal seat/throne (literally "head‑on‑throne" in poetic usage), followed by شاه جهان "king of the world"; together the phrase says the royal throne (or the king on his throne) of the world was overturned.
segments.s685-g135: کس here in a negated sentence functions as "no one." اندر جهان = "in the world." این شگفتی = "this marvel/wonder" (شگفتی as noun). ندید is the past tense "saw" (negated by the context: "no one in the world saw this wonder").
segments.s685-g136: که introduces a subordinate clause "which/that." اکنون = "now/at present." بنوی is a narrative/poetic form meaning roughly "as (I) write" or "I now write" (the provided senses include imperative/optative forms; here it functions as the author's present narration rather than a direct imperative). به ایران رسید is a past/perfective phrase "reached Iran."
segments.s685-g49: چنین گفت is a fixed narrative phrase meaning 'thus said' with گفت in the simple past (3rd sing.). کاین (archaic compound of که + این) introduces the following noun phrase 'that ...' — here کاین پیر دانش‌پژوه names the speaker. Word order is verb + reported subject (poetic/inverted order) rather than subject-verb as in colloquial Persian.
segments.s685-g50: که here introduces a relative clause ('who ...') referring back to پیر. چَندین (quantifier) + سخن (object 'words/speech') + گفت (past 'spoke/said') forms the clause 'who spoke many words'. پیش گروه is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of the group', with پیش meaning 'in the presence of' and گروه 'company/group'. The subject (the پیر) is omitted in the clause because it is resumed by the relative که.
segments.s685-g51: بگویم is a first-person form meaning 'I will say / let me say' (subjunctive/imperative flavor used for 'I will tell'). که introduces the content clause. او از چه گفت این سخن is a clause with an interrogative indirect meaning 'why / about what he said this speech': از چه = 'about what', گفت = past 'said/spoke', and این سخن 'these/the foregoing words' is the object. Note the poetic word order: the object can appear after the verb.
segments.s685-g52: A string of epithets describing the man: جهانجوی (compound adjective 'world-seeking/ambitious') و 'and' داننده ('knowing', a substantive/adjectival agent) followed by the head noun مرد ('man'), with کهن ('old/ancient') following the noun — a common poetic ordering where adjectives may follow the noun. Together they form a nominal phrase modifying 'او' in the previous clause.
segments.s685-g53: که introduces an explanatory clause: این نیکویها 'these virtues/good qualities' is the object. ز + تو is a poetic genitive/oblique construction meaning 'of you' or 'concerning you' (ز here = 'of/from/about'). یاد کرد is a verbal phrase ('made remembrance' = 'remembered'): یاد (remembrance) + کرد (past 'made/did') yields 'remembered'.
segments.s685-g54: دل انجمن is a genitive-like phrase 'the heart of the assembly' (دل = heart, انجمن = assembly). زین سخن is a prepositional/agentive phrase 'by/with this speech' (زین = 'this/by this' in classical usage; note: the supplied senses list for زین does not reflect this function). شاد کرد is the verb phrase 'made glad' (شاد = glad, کرد = made) with the verb at the end in standard Persian order.
segments.s685-g55: ولیکن is a formal/poetic adversative 'but'. یکی modifies داستانست (داستان + است contracted) to mean 'one tale' or 'a certain tale'. نغز is an adjective 'fine/delightful/pithy' qualifying داستان, placed after it in poetic diction but understood as its modifier.
segments.s685-g56: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). بشنود is 3rd person singular present/subjunctive of شنیدن used in conditions to mean 'should hear' or simply 'hear'. مردم 'people' is the subject, and پاک مغز is a compound adjective 'pure-minded / of clean mind' (پاک = pure, مغز = brain/mind). Word order is conditional phrase first, with verb in subjunctive form.
segments.s685-g161: چون introductory 'که' here introduces the content of what is being said ("that"). 'بهرام شاهست' is a nominal clause with the copula -ست attached to 'شاه' meaning 'is king'. 'و ماکهتریم' links another nominal element: 'ماکهتریم' is a proper name (poetic/archaic name) coordinated with the previous clause by 'و'.
segments.s685-g162: This is an object phrase plus verb: 'سر دشمنان را' is the definite object ('the heads of the enemies' marked by را). 'بپی' is an archaic/contracted particle here preceding the verb (an older preverb/particle form), and 'بسپریم' is the 1st person plural present/subjunctive/optative of سپردن ('let us / we will entrust/hand over'). Word order places the object before the verb as is common in Persian.
segments.s685-g163: 'کشیده' is a participial adjective meaning 'drawn' (here modifying 'شمشیر'). 'چو' introduces a temporal clause 'when'. Word order is poetic: 'کشیده' (modifying adjective) + 'چو' + subject 'بهرام' + object 'شمشیر' + verb 'دید' (past 'saw').
segments.s685-g164: Two abstract nouns 'خردمندی و راستی' ('wisdom and uprightness/truth') are presented as the items chosen, followed by the past tense verb 'برگزید' ('he chose'). The verb is simple past 3rd singular; the coordinated nouns function as its objects.
segments.s685-g165: 'چنین گفت کانکو' is a short reported-speech clause: 'چنین گفت' = 'thus he said' and the proper name 'کانکو' is the speaker; the word order is inverted (predicate before name) as a poetic device. 'ز جای نشست' literally 'from his place sat/settled', here idiomatically meaning he left his seat or rose from his place (contextualized by the next clause).
segments.s685-g166: 'برآید' is an older/punctual form meaning 'he rose/stood up' (3rd singular). 'بیازد' is an archaic/contracted verb form (equivalent to بیاندازد / یاخت) meaning 'throws/casts' or 'grips' in context; it combines with 'به شمشیر دست' ('his hand to the sword') where 'به' marks direction/target and 'دست' is the noun 'hand'. Together they describe the sudden motion of seizing the sword.
segments.s685-g167: 'ببرم' is a first-person present/subjunctive/future-like form of بردن ('I will take / I seize'). 'هم' = 'also', and 'اندر زمان' = 'at that time/moment' (a poetic prepositional phrase). 'دست اوی' is 'his hand' where 'اوی' is a possessive third-person enclitic ('his'). The clause expresses a conditional/intended action by the speaker.
segments.s685-g168: 'هشیوار گردد' = 'becomes alert/sober' where 'گردد' is 3rd person singular existential/become (here resultative). 'سر مست اوی' is the subject/affected phrase 'his intoxicated head' (سر = head; مست = intoxicated/figuratively crazed; اوی = 'his'). The order places predicate before this nominal phrase, typical in poetry.
segments.s685-g89: "که تا زنده باشد جهاندار شاه" is a subordinate temporal clause: که + تا introduces 'as long as/while'. باشد is the present/subjunctive form of 'to be' used here in a dependent clause ('is/should be'); جهاندار شاه is an epithet phrase 'world‑ruling king' (compound noun), with the title following the epithet.
segments.s685-g90: "نباشد سپهبد سزاوار گاه" is a negated predicative clause. نباشد is the negative of 'to be' (impersonal/optative sense 'there is not / is not'), with سپهبد as the logical subject/topic; سزاوار گاه = 'fit/deserving of the post/time' (گاه here = 'occasion/office'). The verb precedes its noun in this poetic word order, yielding 'there is no spahbed worthy of the office.'
segments.s685-g91: "وگر بیم داری ز خسرو به دل" is a conditional clause: وگر = 'if'; بیم داری = 'you have fear' (داری = 2nd‑person present 'have'); ز خسرو marks the object of the fear ('of Khosrow'); به دل is a locative phrase 'in your heart/mind'. The clause literally reads 'if you have fear of Khosrow in (your) heart.'
segments.s685-g92: "پی از پارس وز طیسفون برگسل" is a compressed, poetic instruction/description. پی + از ... normally signals motion/retreat 'away from/leave', here 'پی از پارس' = 'leave/away from Pars'; وز is used as 'and/from' in the parallel phrase (poetically written) introducing 'طیسفون' (Ctesiphon); برگسل is an image 'leaf‑scattering (wind)' used as a simile/adverbial to indicate swift, gust‑like flight. Word order and elisions are typical of epic verse, yielding 'depart Pars and Ctesiphon like a leaf‑scattering gust.'
segments.s685-g93: "بشهر خراسان تن آسان بزی" = به شهر خراسان + (تو) تنِ آسان بزی. بشهر (contracted به شهر) = 'to/in the city (of)'; خراسان is the place; تن آسان بزی literally 'you live with an easy body' (آسان modifying تن), i.e. 'live comfortably.' The final form بزی is the verbal form 'you live' in this poetic register (verb at end).
segments.s685-g94: "که آسانی و مهتری را سزی" is a result/explanatory clause introduced by که = 'for/that'; آسانی و مهتری 'ease and preeminence' is marked by را (definite/object/topic marker) and is the semantic patient/beneficiary of the verb سزی, here an archaic/poetic verb meaning 'befit / suit.' The clause therefore says that 'ease and preeminence suit you.'
segments.s685-g95: "به پوزش یک اندر دگر نامه ساز" is an imperative/advice: ساز is an imperative 'make/compose' addressed to 'you' (verb at end); به پوزش = 'for apology' or 'as an apology'; یک اندر دگر نامه = 'one other/another letter' (literally 'one in another letter' — a poetic way to say 'another letter'). The whole phrase is 'compose another letter of apology.'
segments.s685-g96: "مگر خسرو آید برای تو باز" is the qualifying clause introduced by مگر = 'unless/except if'; خسرو آید = 'Khosrow comes/arrives'; برایِ تو باز places the action relative to 'you' and باز = 'again/back' (adverb). Together: 'unless Khosrow comes back for you.'
segments.s685-g17: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that'). زیبا بود is a past copula construction ('was beautiful/fair'); بخشش و بخت را is the phrase being described as 'beautiful' (بخشش = gift-giving, بخت = fortune/fate). The final را is the definite/object marker applied to the phrase being praised.
segments.s685-g18: This lists what was bestowed: کلاه و کمر بستن uses the infinitival/masdar-like بستن ('to bind/put on') governing 'hat and girdle' (کلاه و کمر). وتخت را continues the list ('and the throne') with را marking it as definite/object; the verb بستن is understood to apply to these items.
segments.s685-g19: که دارید begins a rhetorical question ('what do you have' / 'what right have you'). The second که introduces the subordinate clause. اکنون is an adverb 'now', and ببندد is a 3rd-person (present/subjunctive-like) form meaning 'may/should bind or intervene'; میان means 'between'.
segments.s685-g20: بجا آورد is a light-verb compound meaning 'to put in place / to restore' (بجا as adverbial element + آورد 'bring/cause'). رسم و راه are the objects 'custom and way', and کیان names the royal house/kings whose custom/way is meant.
segments.s685-g21: بدارنده is an active participial noun 'holder/possessor'. آفتاب بلند is the noun + adjective 'the high/lofty sun' forming an epithet; the adjective follows the noun, as normal in Persian.
segments.s685-g22: که introduces purpose/result ('so that / that'). باشم is 1st-singular present/subjunctive meaning 'that I may be'. شما را uses را as an object/dative marker here ('to you'). بدین is a prepositional phrase 'by/with this' (ب + این), and یارمند is the noun 'ally/helper' — together: 'that I may be to you, by this, a helper.'
segments.s685-g23: شنیدند is 3rd-person plural past 'they heard'. گردنکشان is the nominative noun 'the rebels/proud-necked ones' acting as the subject, and این سخن is the direct object 'this speech/these words' (this phrase is what they heard).
segments.s685-g24: که introduces the reported content ('that'). آن نامور مهتر is a noun phrase 'that renowned chief' (آن = that; نامور = famed; مهتر = chief/elder). افکند is the past 3rd-singular 'he cast/threw/overthrew', and بن is the object 'base/foundation' — together: 'that that famed chief cast down the foundation.'
segments.s685-g81: بدو گفت is literal 'to him (بدو) [he] said (گفت)': the dative/oblique pronoun precedes the verb, a common poetic order. اکنون که introduces a subordinate clause ('now that' / 'now when'). چندین سخن is the noun phrase 'several/many words/verses' (object of the subordinate clause) and will be specified by the following segment.
segments.s685-g82: سراینده (the performer/poet) and its modifiers follow to identify the source of those 'سخن' (appositive/agent). برنا (young, vigorous) and مرد کهن (an old man) form a coordinated description — 'the youthful singer and the aged man' — with و linking the two nouns; word order places the identifying phrase after the noun it specifies, a frequent poetic pattern.
segments.s685-g83: سرانجام is an adverb 'finally/at last' setting temporal frame. اگر introduces a conditional 'if'; راه جویی is a compressed expression meaning 'you seek a way' (راه = way; جویی here nominal/Verbal noun 'search' or a 2nd-person present-form used poetically). بداد appears in the text where one would expect a verb form meaning 'give' or similar, but the provided senses do not match the verbal use, so its semantic index is marked -1.
segments.s685-g84: This is an optative clause ending in باد ('may it be'). هیونی ('a beast' / savage creature) is the subject; برافگن acts as a descriptive/verbal adjective 'ablaze; kindled' and بکردار is a predicate adjective 'virtuous / of good character'. The sequence sets two wishes/predications about that subject: may the beast be blazing and virtuous.
segments.s685-g85: ممان is an archaic/poetic negative-imperative or hortative root meaning 'do not remain/withhold/delay' (used here as a prohibition). دیر is an adverb 'long' (of time). تا introduces the subordinate temporal clause 'until' that specifies the endpoint: خسرو سرفراز (Khosrow, the triumphant) is the subject of that following clause.
segments.s685-g86: بکوبد is a 3rd-person singular verb (here used in the sense 'he will come/strike/arrive' in context; the form is poetically archaic). بنزد 'to (the presence of)' places the action toward someone — here 'near you' (تو). راه دراز is an adverbial/locative phrase 'by/along the long road' specifying the route of his arrival.
segments.s685-g87: ز کار گذشته is an ablative phrase 'from/away from past deeds' (ز = from; کار گذشته = what is past/done). به پوزش گرای is an imperative-like construction 'turn toward pardon/seek forgiveness' where به marks direction ('toward') and گرای indicates turning or inclination (here 'incline yourself to / turn to').
segments.s685-g88: سوی تخت گستاخ is a prepositional phrase 'toward the presumptuous/insolent throne' (سوی = toward; تخت گستاخ = arrogant throne). مگذار پای is a negative imperative 'do not place/set (مگذار) foot (پای)' — literally 'do not set foot' toward that throne; word order places the negative verb before the object noun.
segments.s685-g25: نپیچید is a past negative verb (ن + پیچید) meaning 'did not turn/deviate' with third-person singular form. کس after the verb with this negation yields an indefinite-negative sense 'no one' (literally: 'no one's heart'). دل is the inner subject ('heart/heartfelt resolve') and ز + گفتار راست means 'from the straight/true speech' (ز = 'from' in poetic register; گفتار = 'speech'; راست = 'truth/straightness'). The clause overall: 'No one's heart turned away from the truthful/straight words.'
segments.s685-g26: یکی here is indefinite 'a certain' (introducing someone). پیرتر is the comparative/adjective 'older' (an elder). بود is the past copula 'was' used here as the background verb, and بر پای خاست is a fixed phrase 'rose to (his) feet' where بر = 'onto/upon', پای = 'foot', and خاست = 'arose' (past). Word order is subject (یکی پیرتر) + copula + verbal phrase.
segments.s685-g27: کجا is an interrogative/exclamatory 'where' used rhetorically. نام او is the noun phrase 'his name/reputation' serving as the subject/topic, with بود the past copula 'was'. شهران گراز is a reversed predicate: گراز ('boar' literally) qualified by شهران ('of the cities' or 'among the cities'), i.e. 'the cities' boar' (a figurative epithet). The clause reads roughly as a rhetorical question/assertion about how he was famed among the towns.
segments.s685-g28: گوی here is the reporting verb/particle 'they say' / 'so it is said' (an archaic poetic form standing before the speaker or reported remark). پیرسر is a compound adjective 'gray‑headed' / 'old‑headed' describing the speaker (an elder). مهتری is an appositive noun 'preeminence' or 'the preeminent one' and دیریاز qualifies it as 'long‑standing' or 'long ago'. The three words form an attributive phrase naming or describing the elder who speaks or is being cited.
segments.s685-g29: چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said' in narrative past). کای is the vocative particle 'O' (classical/poetic) attached to the addressee; نامدار بلند are epithets 'renowned' and 'lofty/loftily high' placed after the vocative to address the person respectfully. Word order: report verb + direct speech introduced by the vocative and attributive adjectives.
segments.s685-g30: This is a compressed, poetic clause describing the addressee's quality in the world. توی (here an archaic/poetic form related to 'تو' or the prepositional 'in') + در جهان = 'in the world' and تابوی سودمند should be read poetically as تابِ وی (the radiance/splendour of him) + سودمند = 'a beneficial/advantageous radiance'. In other words, 'you are, in the world, a helpful/beneficial splendour.' Note: تابوی is an older poetic contraction (تابِ + وی) rather than the modern loanword 'تابو', so its appearance is archaic.
segments.s685-g31: بدی‌گر is a noun 'evildoer/malefactor'. نبودی is the past negative existential/coprocal 'there was not/was no', so together (نبودی بدی‌گر) gives 'there was no evildoer'. جز از = 'except from/of' introduces the exception; ساوه شاه is the proper name + title 'the king of Saveh'. Word order is classical/poetic but straightforward: 'There was no evildoer except the king of Saveh.'
segments.s685-g32: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing a relative clause. آمد is the past 'came'. بدین should be read as the prepositional compound به + این 'to this', here written as بدین (classical spelling); مرز ما = 'our border/frontier' (مرز = border, ما = our/us). با سپاه = 'with an army' (با = with, سپاه = army). The clause: 'who came to this our border with an army.'
segments.s685-g105: خردمند is the subject 'the wise man.' نپسندد is a negative present/present‑subjunctive third‑person verb 'does not approve / will not approve.' این گفت وگوی is the object 'this conversation' (گفت و گو = conversation) placed after the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s685-g106: کزان is a causal/relative particle 'from which / because of which.' کم شود is a verbal phrase 'becomes less / is diminished' (کم = less; شود = becomes). مرد را ... روی is the object phrase with the accusative marker را attached to مرد ('the man's ...'); آب روی (written here with 붙together) means the man's reputation/face, so the clause means 'from which the man's reputation is reduced.'
segments.s685-g107: خراسان here denotes the Khorasani man/one from Khorasan (subject). سخن برمنش وار is a noun phrase 'speech in a noble/gentle manner' where برمنش 'noble‑minded' combines with the suffix وار '‑like / in the manner of.' گفت is the verb 'said / spoke' (past), with object preceding the verb in this poetic line.
segments.s685-g108: نگویم is first person negative present/subjunctive 'I will not say / I do not say.' که introduces the content clause. آن با خرد بود جفت is an inverted predication: آن = 'that,' با خرد = 'with wisdom,' بود = past 'was,' and جفت = 'a match / counterpart'; together: 'that was a match with wisdom' (here negated by نگویم).
segments.s685-g109: فرخ‌زاد is the proper name subject. بفزود is a classical past tense verb 'added / increased.' گفتار تند is the object 'harsh / sharp speech' (تند = sharp/harsh here). The verb governs the following noun phrase.
segments.s685-g110: دل مردم is the possessed noun 'the hearts (minds) of the people' with مردم following دل. پرخرد is a predicate adjective 'full of wisdom / very wise.' کرد is the causative/past 'made' and کند is an auxiliary/modal present form used poetically (lit. 'made (so) that ... become'); together they express 'caused the people's hearts to become wise.'
segments.s685-g111: چهارم is 'the fourth' (ordinal) modifying خزروان 'the Khzrawan / the kings/royalty.' سالاربود is a fused predicate 'was a sālar / was commander' (classical compound meaning 'served as chief' or 'was a leader'), so the clause says 'he was the fourth of the Khzrawan leaders.'
segments.s685-g112: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'that / who.' گفتار او = 'his speech.' با خرد یاربود is a small clause 'was a companion/ally with wisdom' (با خرد = 'with wisdom,' یار بود = 'was a friend/ally of'). Together: 'who's speech was allied with wisdom.'
segments.s685-g113: که تا here introduces a temporal clause meaning 'from the time that / when'. آفرید is a past (perfect) verb 'created' (3rd person singular). The subject کردگار ('the Creator') follows the verb for poetic inversion, and این جهان is the direct object ('this world').
segments.s685-g114: پدید آمد is a compound verb meaning 'appeared / came into being' (past). این گردش روزگار is the noun phrase serving as the logical subject 'this turning/cycle of time'; گردش here means 'revolution/turning' and روزگار 'time' or 'the course of time'. The verb–then–subject order is a poetic inversion.
segments.s685-g115: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of' used in a fronted phrase; ضحاک تازی is an apposition 'Zahhāk the Arab'. نخست 'first' indicates temporal priority. اندرآی is an archaic past-form verb 'entered / came among' (3rd person singular).
segments.s685-g116: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'who/that'. بیدادگر is a descriptive noun/adjective 'tyrant, oppressor'. بود is the past copula 'was' (3rd person). و links the two predicates; ناپاک رای is a noun phrase 'unclean/corrupt counsel' (ناپاک qualifying رای).
segments.s685-g117: که again introduces the clause. جمشید is the subject. برتر منش is a compound epithet 'of superior/supreme origin/character' (برتر modifying منش). را is the definite object marker. بکشت is an archaic past verb form (3rd person) meaning 'struck/killed' (poetic past of کشتن); word order places verb at the end of this clause.
segments.s685-g118: به here functions adverbially 'by/with' (marking the means). بیداد means 'injustice/oppression' and together به بیداد modifies the verb that follows. بگرفت is a past verb 'seized / took hold of' (3rd person). گیتی is 'the world' (direct object of بگرفت). بمشت is a compacted poetic phrase meaning 'into (his) fist / in his grasp' (literally 'to/into fist'); its exact analytic senses were not supplied in the list.
segments.s685-g119: پر از درد is a prepositional phrase 'full of pain' (پر 'full' + از 'of' + درد 'pain/sorrow') placed before the verb for emphasis. دیدم is the first-person past 'I saw'. دل پارسا is the noun phrase 'the pious heart' (دل = heart; پارسا = pious person/one). The overall order is topicalized for emphasis.
segments.s685-g120: که introduces the explanatory subordinate clause 'for / because'. اندر جهان is locative 'in the world'. دیو بد پادشا is a terse nominal phrase 'a demon, an evil king' describing the ruler; the copula (بود 'was') is omitted here for metrical/poetic reasons, so this stands as a verbless clause meaning 'there was an evil demon-king in the world.'
segments.s685-g33: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of'; 'ز آزادگان بندگان' means 'servants from the free' (construction: source + noun + patient). 'خواست کرد' is a periphrastic past (خواست + کرد) meaning 'demanded/asked for' — 'خواست' expresses the desire/request and 'کرد' is the auxiliary 'did', so the whole phrase = 'he demanded'.
segments.s685-g34: This is a locative-existential clause: 'کجا' = 'where'; 'در جهانش' = 'in his world/realm' (جهان + possessive suffix -ش). 'نبد' is an archaic negative copula 'was not / there was not'. 'هم‌نبرد' here means 'an equal/rival in battle'. The clause therefore: 'where in his realm there was no equal in battle.'
segments.s685-g35: ز گیتی functions as a circumstantial phrase 'in the world' (ز used poetically). 'بمردی' is an adverbial/qualifying phrase 'with manhood / manly courage'. 'تو' is the subject 'you', and 'بستی میان' (بستی + میان) = 'you closed (the gap) / you placed yourself between' — i.e., you interposed yourself.
segments.s685-g36: که introduces a result clause 'so that/so' here. 'آن رنج بگذشت' = 'that suffering passed / was removed' where بگذشت is past 'passed by/away'. 'ز ایرانیان' is an ablative 'from the Iranians' — 'that suffering passed away from the Iranians.'
segments.s685-g37: This is a compressed enumerative phrase: 'سپه' = 'the army/host'; 'چاربار' = 'four times/quadruple' (adverbial qualifier); 'از یلان' = 'of/consisting of heroes' (از indicates composition); 'صدهزار' is the numeral 'one hundred thousand' modifying the host — i.e. the army amounted to a hundred thousand heroes (emphatically fourfold).
segments.s685-g38: همه = 'all/everyone'; گرد here is used in the sense 'around/together' (poetic grouping) giving 'all assembled/together'; و = 'and'; شایسته = 'worthy/fit'; کارزار = 'battle' (i.e. 'fit for battle'). So the phrase states that every one was gathered and fit for the fight.
segments.s685-g39: بیک here is an archaic/poetic element (here read as a title/word preceding the phrase); 'چوبه تیر' = 'shaft (of the) arrow' (چوبه = frame/shaft + تیر = arrow). 'تو' = 'your'; 'گشتند' = 'they turned/they were turned' (past of گشتن, here 'turned back'); 'باز' = 'again' — together: 'at the shaft of your arrow they turned back again.'
segments.s685-g40: برآسود is a predicate meaning 'was at rest / was relieved'. 'ایران' is the subject/place. 'ز' = 'from'; 'گرم و گداز' are paired ablatives 'heat and burning/ardor' (گرم = warm/hot, گداز = melting/ardor) meaning 'from heat and anguish' — i.e., Iran was relieved of its suffering.
segments.s685-g57: This whole clause is a reporting phrase. که introduces reported speech ('that'); زر دشت is a proper name (Zar‑Dasht) composed of زر + دشت; گوید is the 3rd‑person present 'he says' used to introduce what follows; باستا و زند are quoted titles/words (Basta and Zend) following the verb. Word order is Subject (زر دشت) → Verb (گوید) → quoted items, typical in classical Persian.
segments.s685-g58: This begins the content of the report with a subordinate 'that' (که). هرکس 'whoever/anyone' is the indefinite relative; the second que (که) is the relative/connector often repeated for emphasis; از is the preposition 'from/of'; کردگاربلند is a compound 'کردگار' (Creator/Doer) with the adjective بلند 'high/exalted' following as in classical order. The clause is an incomplete relative clause leading into the following lines.
segments.s685-g59: بپیچد is a 3rd‑person jussive/conditional form of پیچیدن (prefixed ب‑), here functioning like 'should he turn/if he stray(s)'. بیک سال is an archaic phrase meaning 'for one year' (بیک = یک), and پندش ('pends + ‑ش') is 'his advice' with the enclitic ‑ش = 'him/his'. دهید is the 2nd‑person plural imperative or present subjunctive 'give'. Together: 'If he strays, give him advice for a year.'
segments.s685-g60: همان is a demonstrative 'the same/that same'; مایه here means 'amount/resource' (i.e., allowance); سودمندش is the adjective 'useful/profitable' + enclitic ‑ش = 'to him' (so 'useful to him'); دهید again is 'give'. The clause repeats and specifies what to give: 'give him that same useful sum.'
segments.s685-g61: سرسال is an adverbial time expression 'after a year / at the year's turn' (classical usage). اگر introduces the condition 'if'; بازناید is a negative present/future form meaning 'does not/will not return' (باز + ناید); به راه is the prepositional phrase 'to the road/way' meaning 'to the right way/return to the path.' Together they form the protasis: 'if after a year he does not return to the way...'
segments.s685-g62: ببایدش is the impersonal/modal construction 'it must be (of him)' with the enclitic ‑ش = 'him' (so 'he must be...'); کشتن is the verbal noun 'killing' used here as the complement to باید ('he must be killed'); بفرمان 'by the order of' is a prepositional phrase qualifying the killing; شاه 'the king' is the agent. The literal order is 'it must be of him killing by order of the king' → 'he must be killed by the king's order.'
segments.s685-g63: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when/if'; بر is the preposition 'against/to' used with دشمن 'enemy' to mean 'an enemy to'; دادگر modifies شاه and means 'just' or 'just ruler' (compound noun/adjective before شاه); شود is the 3rd‑person present 'becomes'. Word order is typical: temporal conjunction → prepositional object → verb ('when he becomes an enemy to the just king').
segments.s685-g64: سرش is 'his head' (سر + enclitic ‑ش = his). زود 'soon' modifies the predicate. باید که is the necessary construction 'it must be that…' and بی‌تن (literally 'without body') is a euphemism meaning 'beheaded' or 'made bodyless'; شود 'becomes/is made' completes the clause. The clause states the consequence: 'his head must soon be made bodyless.'
segments.s685-g121: دگر آنک is a temporal/relative opening: دگر 'again' (here = 'once more') + آنک as a relative/conjunctive 'that/who/when.' بد گوهر is a poetic epithet with بد (negative/evil) preceding گوهر (here 'essence' rather than literal jewel) to mean 'of evil nature,' and it directly qualifies the following proper name افراسیاب.
segments.s685-g122: ز توران is a simple prepositional phrase 'from Turan' (ز = 'from'). بدانگونه is an adverb 'in that manner/thus.' بگذاشت is a past verbal form (b- prefix + گذاشت) meaning 'he left/let/caused to be left' in older/poetic usage; here it takes آب ('water') as its object, placed after the verb as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s685-g123: بزاری here functions adverbially ('with lamentation' / 'lamenting') derived from زاری/زاریدن. سر نوذر نامدار is a noun phrase 'the head of Nowzar the famed' with سر ('head') followed by the proper name نوذر and the epithet نامدار; Persian often strings noun + name + epithet in this order.
segments.s685-g124: بشمشیر is an instrumental phrase 'with the sword.' ببرید is a past action here ('cut off' / 'they cut'); although the lemma list is limited, the clause means the cutting action was done by the sword. و برگشت کار is a short clause meaning 'and the affair/situation turned back' — برگشت as 'reversal' with کار 'affair' indicating that the situation changed.
segments.s685-g125: سدیگر is an introductory marker 'another/the other' (introducing a new example). سکندر is the proper name 'Alexander.' که introduces a relative clause 'who came,' آمد is simple past 'came,' and ز روم marks origin 'from Rum/Byzantium.'
segments.s685-g126: به ایران is the directional phrase 'to/into Iran.' و links the clauses; ویران شد is a resultative/periphrastic expression 'was ruined/laid waste' (ویران = ruined, شد = became/was), and این مرز وبوم ('this frontier and homeland') is the affected subject placed after the verb for poetic inversion.
segments.s685-g127: چو is the temporal subordinating particle 'when.' دارای شمشیر زن here is a compact description 'the one possessing a sword' = 'the sword‑wielder' (دارای + noun phrase), and را marks that phrase as the definite object of بکشت — an older past form of 'to kill' (کشتن) whose subject is omitted but understood from context. In short: 'when [he] killed/when [he] was killed' (context shows a killing took place involving the sword‑wielder).
segments.s685-g128: خور و خواب is a conventional pair 'eating and sleeping' (خور here as eating/food rather than 'sun'), ایرانیان is the subject 'the Iranians,' شد is the copular/resultative 'became,' and درشت is a predicate adjective 'coarse/harsh.' The clause states that the Iranians' basic sustenance and rest became rough/hard.
segments.s685-g41: کنون (konun) is an adverb meaning “now/presently” and sits at the start to set the time. تخت ایران is a noun phrase in the normal modifier-head order (تخت = throne, modified by ایران = of Iran). سزاوار تست is a predicate: سزاوار (fit, deserving) governs a following copula; تست here is an archaic/copular element (= هست / “is”), so the clause means “the throne of Iran is worthy/fit for you.”
segments.s685-g42: برین is the preposition بر + the demonstrative این (here spelled ین) = “upon this/on this.” برگوا is an archaic verbal/item whose exact modern reflex is uncertain but functions to link the prepositional phrase to the following nominal clause. بخت is the grammatical subject (“fortune/fate”) and بیدارتست is a fused predicate: بیدار = “awake” + تست (archaic copula) = “is awake,” so the clause reads “upon this your fortune is awake.”
segments.s685-g43: کسی کو is an archaic relative construction meaning “anyone who / whoever” (کسی = someone; کو here functions like که/«who»). بپیچد is the present/subjunctive form of پیچیدن “to turn/twist,” here “turns aside” or “deviates.” ز فرمان ما is a prepositional phrase (ز = from, فرمان ما = our command/decree), the source from which one turns.
segments.s685-g44: وگر is a conditional/concessive marker “and if / but if.” دور ماند is a verbal phrase where دور (distant) combines with ماند (from ماندن, “to remain / be left”) to mean “becomes/ remains distant.” ز پیمان ما repeats the prepositional source “from our pact/covenant.” Together it expresses an if-clause: “and if he stays away from our covenant.”
segments.s685-g45: بفرمانش is بِ + فرمان + ش (enclitic “his”) = “by his order / at his command.” آریم is an archaic first-person-plural present/subjunctive form of آوردن/آوردن-like verbs meaning “we bring / we will bring / we do.” اگر چه گوست is a concessive phrase: اگر چه = “although / though,” and گوست (archaic impersonal) = “it is said / they say,” so: “we will do it by his order, although it is said...”
segments.s685-g46: و‌گر again = “and if / but if.” داستان را uses را as a topical/object marker on داستان (the tale/account) to focus it. همه modifies داستان as “the whole/entire tale.” خسروست is a predicate with the copular -ست = “is (Khosrow’s / belonging to the king)” so the clause reads roughly “and if the whole tale belongs to the king / is the king’s.”
segments.s685-g47: بگفت is the simple past third-person “he said.” این refers back to the preceding words (“this”), و = “and,” بنشست is past “he sat down.” بر جای خویش is a locative phrase: بر = on/at, جای = place, and the enclitic ش (attached to خوی) forms خویش = “his own,” so “he sat down in his own place.”
segments.s685-g48: خراسان سپهبد is a compound title where the region precedes the office: “the spahbed (general) of Khorasan.” بیامد is the past/preterite “came,” and به پیش is a direction/position phrase (به = to/toward, پیش = before / into the presence of) meaning “came forward / came into the presence (of the court).”
segments.s685-g9: This phrase sets up a comparison: ز شاهان = 'of/from the kings' (poetic genitive), then ز ضحاک = 'than Zahhak' and بتر is the comparative 'more terrible/worse'. کسی is the indefinite 'anyone/someone' that will be negated by the following verb, so the whole chunk means 'of the kings no one worse than Zahhak...'. Word order (genitive first, then the standard noun) is typical of classical Persian.
segments.s685-g10: نیامد is the negative past 'did not come / did not appear'. پدیدار is a noun 'appearance, emergence' used as the verbal complement ('did not appear'). بجویی is the infinitival/nominal idea of 'seeking' (here 'in search of / seeking'); بسی = 'much / many' (literary). Together: 'did not appear seeking much' i.e. 'no one more terrible than Zahhak appeared who sought many (victims)'.
segments.s685-g11: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. از بهر = 'for the sake of / because of' (classical prepositional phrase), شاهی here means 'kingship' (the goal). پدر را is the definite object 'his father' marked by را, and بکشت is the archaic past-form verb 'killed' (classical b- prefix). The clause reads 'who killed his father for the sake of kingship.'
segments.s685-g12: وزان = 'and/from that' (referring to the killing just mentioned). کشتن is the verbal noun 'that killing'. ایرانش = 'his Iran' or 'Iran' as the affected entity; آمد is the simple past 'came'. بمشت is a compact idiom 'into the fist/hand' (poetic: 'into his power'). The line means 'and from that killing Iran came into his fist' (i.e. Iran fell under his control).
segments.s685-g13: دگر here means 'another' or 'other'; خسرو is a title 'king/sovereign'. آن مرد = 'that man', and the epithets بیداد و شوم are attributive: بیداد 'oppressive/tyrannical' and شوم 'ill‑omened / accursed'. Together they identify the person as 'another king, that tyrannical and ill‑omened man.'
segments.s685-g14: پدر را بکشت repeats 'he killed (his) father' (پدر را = object + بکشت = past 'killed'). آنگهی = 'thereupon/then', شد = 'became' (or 'it happened'), and بروم is an infinitival/subjunctive form of رفتن 'to go' used here idiomatically: 'شد بروم' functions as 'then he rose / came to be' (i.e. rose to power or set out). The clause thus says 'he killed his father; thereupon he rose.'
segments.s685-g15: کنون = 'now/presently'. ناپدیدست is the literary fused form ناپدید است 'is disappeared / has vanished'. اندر جهان = 'in the world'. Together: 'now has vanished from the world'.
segments.s685-g16: یکی = 'one / a certain' (introduces an individual). نامداری is a noun meaning 'a renowned/notable one' (a famed person). ز تخت مهان = 'from the throne of the great / from the royal throne' (ز = 'from/of', تخت = 'throne'; مهان is a poetic word for great ones/nobles here). The phrase identifies 'a certain famed person of the royal throne' who has disappeared.
segments.s685-g73: ببهرام (be + بهرَم) is a prepositional phrase 'to/for Bahram' placed before the verb; گوید is the narrative present used like a past-tense 'said' in epic Persian (he says/said). که introduces the reported content ('that'). نوشه بدی is a noun (نوشه, 'draught, sip') followed by the adjectival/qualifying بدی ('bad/evil'), the normal Persian order noun + modifier here meaning 'a bad draught.'
segments.s685-g74: جهان را: جهان 'the world' with را marking it as the definite/object (emphatic) accusative. بدیدار here denotes 'meeting/visit' (the idea of an encounter, often with death in Persian poetry). توشه بدی is a compound idea 'provision/pack (توشه) that is bad (بدی)' — i.e. 'a poor/evil provision'; word order places the object (جهان) before the verbal/nominal phrase describing the provision for the meeting.
segments.s685-g75: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). ناپسندست is an adjectival predicate meaning 'displeasing/objectionable' and governs گفتار ما ('our speech/words') where گفتار is the noun 'speech' and ما is the possessive pronoun 'our/us.' The phrase thus reads 'if our speech is objectionable...'.
segments.s685-g76: بدین is an adverbial/demonstrative sense 'by this/in this way' formed from the prefix ب- with دین in older usage; نیست is the negative copula 'is not.' پیروزگر یارما is a nominal phrase 'our victorious/yet-conquering friend' (پیروزگر 'victorious, triumphant' modifying یارما 'our friend/companion' where یارـ ما is the possessive compound). Syntactically the negative copula follows the adverbial, yielding 'it is not thus/so toward our victorious friend.'
segments.s685-g77: انوشه بدی here appears as a proper name or epithet (Anoushe‑bad/Anoushe‑badi); شاد is an optative/adjectival 'happy, rejoice' and تاجاودان is a compound noun 'everlasting crown' (تاج + آباد/جاودان 'eternal') forming a blessing or wish 'may he be glad in the eternal crown.' Word order places the name before the wish/attribute.
segments.s685-g78: زتو is the prepositional phrase 'from you' or 'away from you.' دور is an adjective 'far' modifying دست و زبان ('hand and tongue'), a fixed pair meaning 'deeds and words' or 'action and speech.' ب + دان here produces the older fused form 'بدان' functioning as an instruction/command sense 'keep (them) so / let them be so' — overall: 'let hand and tongue be far from you' i.e. 'do not touch or speak (of him).'
segments.s685-g79: بگفت is the narrative past/present 'he said' (common epic usage), این 'this' is the object (what was said), و 'and' links clauses, بنشست is the past tense 'sat down.' The order is verb then subject noun phrase 'مرد دلیر' (mard-e dilir 'the brave man') following the verb, which is a normal narrative ordering in classical Persian literature.
segments.s685-g80: خزروان is a royal title/collective noun ('royalty/kings') used here in apposition to خسرو (Khosrow), so the phrase reads 'Khosrow, the king/sovereign.' بیامد is the past 'came,' and چو شیر is a simile 'like a lion' (چو = like/as, شیر = lion). The clause describes Khosrow's entrance in vivid epic style.
segments.s685-g97: نه + verb is a plain negation: نه برداشت is a past-tense negation (‘did not lift/take’). برداشت is the past of برداشتن used here as ‘lift/move’. The subject خسرو follows the verb (typical flexible Persian word order). پی here means ‘a foot/step’, and از جای خویش is a prepositional phrase ‘from his place’ with ش (the enclitic) marking the possessive.
segments.s685-g98: کجا begins a rhetorical question ‘where’. نهد is the present/simple third-person of نهاد (here ‘to place/set’), used in a general/rhetorical sense (‘could place’). زاد فرخ functions as an epithet (‘the fortunate-born’), with زاد relating to birth/origin and فرخ as ‘fortunate’. پای پیش = ‘a foot forward’ (پیش = forward/before).
segments.s685-g99: سخن گفت is a simple past clause ‘spoke/said’. پس is temporal ‘then/after that’. زاد فرخ بداد here should be read as a run of names/epithets (no commas in the original), so these words function as the subject/name of the speaker rather than as ordinary common nouns or verbs.
segments.s685-g100: که‌ای is a vocative/attention formula (که + ای = ‘O…’); نامداران is the vocative/plural ‘renowned ones’. فرخ نژاد is an adjectival phrase ‘fortunate-born / of fortunate stock’ (فرخ = ‘fortunate’ modifying نژاد = ‘lineage’), addressing the addressees as of fortunate descent.
segments.s685-g101: شنیدم is first-person past ‘I heard’. سخن گفتن is a verbal noun/infinitive construction ‘the speaking of words’ or simply ‘speak/uttering’ and functions as the direct object of شنیدم. مهتران (‘elders/chiefs’) is the logical subject of that verbal phrase (sense not given in the provided list, hence treated as a special noun).
segments.s685-g102: که introduces the reported clause ‘that’. هستند is the present plural copula ‘are’. ز is the poetic variant of از ‘from’. گزیده modifies سران: گزیده سران = ‘the chosen leaders’, with گزیده as an adjective before the noun.
segments.s685-g103: نخستین is an adverb/adjectival phrase ‘first’. سخن گفتن is again the verbal noun ‘to speak/speaking’. بنده وار is a manner phrase: بنده is a humble self-reference (‘your servant’) and وار is the suffix meaning ‘-like’, so بنده‌وار = ‘in a humble/servant-like manner’. Together: ‘first, speaking humbly…’.
segments.s685-g104: که تا introduces purpose/result (‘so that / in order that’). پهلوانی is the abstract/role ‘heroism/being a champion’. شود is the present/subjunctive of شدن used to express potential/result ‘may become’ or ‘become’. شهریار (the subject after the verb) = ‘the sovereign/king’: literal order is verb then subject (typical in classical poetry).
segments.s685-g65: خراسان (a proper name) is the subject. بگفت is an archaic past form meaning “said” (verb, 3rd sing.). The object این (“this”) follows the verb here in the phrase بگفت این = “said this,” and و links the clause to the following action; لب راببست is an object (لب) + direct-object marker را fused (راببست) with the past verb بَست — literally “he shut the lip(s).” Note the older orthography/compounding (راببست) where classical Persian places the object before the verb.
segments.s685-g66: بیامد is a past-tense verb ‘he came’. بجایی is here a compound of به + جای meaning “to the place/seat,” used adverbially (the provided lemma senses do not include this exact locative sense). که introduces a relative clause “where/that,” and بودش is بود + enclitic pronoun (–ش) = “where he had been / which was his.” نشست is the past tense ‘sat (down).’ The overall sequence gives: he came to the place where he had been and sat.
segments.s685-g67: ازان پس is a temporal phrase “thereupon/after that.” فرخ‌زاد is the proper name (subject). برپای خاست is an idiomatic combination: برپای = “to/onto his feet” and خاست is the past verb ‘arose/stood up.’ Word order places the adverbial/locative phrase before the verb, as typical in classical Persian.
segments.s685-g68: ازان here repeats the temporal/deictic ‘thereupon/from that.’ انجمن = “assembly/gathering” is the source phrase; سر برآورد is an idiom meaning ‘he lifted/raised his head’ (سر = head, برآورد literally ‘brought up/raised’). راست qualifies how he raised it: straight/upright. Note that برآورد in older usage here functions as a light verb meaning “lifted up,” even if the lemma senses given are limited.
segments.s685-g69: چنین گفت is a fixed phrase ‘thus he said’ (چنین = thus). کای is the vocative particle “O,” and مهتر سودمند are vocative epithets: مهتر = “chief/elder,” سودمند = “beneficial/benefactor.” The sequence is an address: “Thus he said, ‘O chief, (you) benefactor…’.” Note that گفت here functions as the reporting verb (past); the lexeme senses supplied are nouns (conversation), so the exact verb sense is not in the provided list.
segments.s685-g70: سخن گفتن is an infinitival noun phrase ‘to speak / speaking’ (سخن = speech + گفتن infinitive). داد is the past verb ‘gave’ which in this context commonly means ‘allowed/granted’ (i.e., gave permission). به‌گر is the beneficiary/dative ‘to the benefactor/favourer,’ and پسند is ‘approval/pleasing’ — here finalizing the request as conditional/polite (‘if it pleases [you]’). The word order places the infinitive object before the verb (typical) and the beneficiary after the verb.
segments.s685-g71: اگر introduces a protasis (‘if’). داد here means ‘justice’ (not the verb ‘gave’). بهتر is the comparative ‘better.’ بود is the past/subjunctive copula ‘were/was’ expressing the counterfactual. کس مباد is an optative/negative wish: کس = ‘anyone/person’ used with مباد (‘may it not be’) yields ‘may there be no one.’
segments.s685-g72: که introduces the relative clause ‘who/that.’ باشد is the present/subjunctive form ‘may be / would be’ (subjunctive/optative nuance). به گفتار literally ‘in/with speech’ (به + گفتار) marks the domain (‘in speech’). بی‌داد means ‘without justice’ or ‘unjust,’ and شاد = ‘joyful/delighted.’ Together the clause reads: ‘who would be joyful in speech lacking justice.’
segments.s685-g137: که here introduces a subordinate temporal clause (classical/poetic use = 'when/that'). بگریخت is the past 3rd‑person singular of گریخت (to flee), with the narrative/past prefix بـ common in older Persian. شاهی چوخسرو is an attributive word order (شاهی 'royal' before the name) meaning 'the royal Khosrow'. زگاه is an adverbial meaning 'at that time / at that moment'.
segments.s685-g138: سوی دشمنان شد is a clause where شد (become/turn) functions as the main verb 'turned / came to be toward the enemies'; سوی + دشمنان = 'toward the enemies'. ز introduces an origin/agent phrase 'from' and governs دست سپاه (literally 'the hand of the army'); here ز دست سپاه indicates loss/transfer into the army's (or by the army's) control/hand. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic rhythm.
segments.s685-g139: بگفت is the past narrative verb 'he said' (بـ‑ prefix + گفت). این refers back to the preceding statement ('this' / 'the foregoing'). و = 'and'. بنشست is past 'he sat down'. گریان is a participial/adjectival form 'weeping' modifying the subject; بدرد here conveys 'in pain/sorrow' (an idiomatic pairing: 'گریان بدرد' = 'sat down, weeping in distress').
segments.s685-g140: ز گفتار او is a causal/ablative phrase 'from / because of his speech' (ز = 'from/because of', گفتار = 'speech', او = 'his'). گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/turned', here introducing the resulting state. بهرام is the patient/subject who changes, and زرد is the predicative adjective 'pale/wan'.
segments.s685-g141: جهاندیده is a compound adjective 'world‑seen / experienced' placed before the name; such epithets commonly precede the noun in classical Persian. سنباد here functions as a proper name (rendered as Sanbad / Sinbad). برپای جست is a verbal phrase meaning 'he sprang to his feet' (برپای = 'onto the feet / standing', جست = past 'leaped').
segments.s685-g142: میان بسته is a compact descriptive phrase: میان 'middle' (here 'waist/midsection') and بسته 'bound/tied', so 'with his waist bound'. وتیغ is the fused poetic form of و + تیغ, i.e. 'and a blade/sword'; هندی qualifies the blade as 'Indian'; بدست is a fixed locative phrase 'in (his) hand' (literally 'to/at hand'). Together the phrase sketches his posture and armament.
segments.s685-g143: چنین گفت is the idiom 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت here marks the utterance). کاین is the contracted classical conjunction introducing reported content ('that this' / 'that'), and نامور پهلوان is a noun phrase with the adjective نامور 'renowned' before پهلوان 'champion/hero'. The clause is an appended direct/indirect speech identifying the hero.
segments.s685-g144: بزرگست is the archaic/persianate copular construction 'is great' (‑ست = 'is'); و = 'and'. با introduces qualities possessed by the hero ('with'), here داد = 'justice' (an abstract quality) and روشن‌روان is a compound adjective meaning 'bright‑souled / clear‑spirited'. The sequence lists honorable attributes.
segments.s685-g153: ببرم is first-person singular present/subjunctive (used here with a volitional/future force) of بردن ‘to take’; in context it means 'I will take/cut off'. سرش را is the direct object: سر 'head' + enclitic ش 'his' and the object-marking را. بشمشیر is an older/concentrated form of با + شمشیر = 'with a sword' (instrument), and تیز is the postpositive adjective 'sharp/keen' modifying شمشیر.
segments.s685-g154: زجانش is the prepositional phrase ز + جان + ش, here 'at the cost of his life' (i.e. taking his life). برآرم is first-person singular present/subjunctive of برآوردن 'to lift/raise' meaning 'I will raise'. دم is 'breath'/'blast' (a short, forceful utterance or cry) and رستخیز is the noun 'uprising/resurrection' — together دم رستخیز = 'the cry/blast of uprising' that he will raise using the foe's life.
segments.s685-g155: نمانم is first-person singular negative of ماندن 'to remain' meaning 'I will not remain' — here idiomatically 'I will not permit/allow it to be the case'. که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/so that'; کس means 'anyone' and تاجداری (tāj-dāri) denotes holding the crown/sovereignty. کند is the present/subjunctive third-person singular of کردن used here to express 'may do' or simply 'do' in reported content.
segments.s685-g156: میان is the locative preposition 'among/between' and سواران is the plural 'horsemen/riders'. سورای (from سورا/سورای) means a feast or revelry; کند again is the verb 'to make/do' in the subordinate clause, so میان سواران سورای کند = 'to hold a feast among the horsemen.' The verb is repeated to predicate the second coordinated action.
segments.s685-g157: چو بشنید (here written چوبشنید) is the temporal clause marker چو 'when' + past verb بشنید 'he heard' — i.e. 'when he heard'. بابوی appears as a proper name or epithet (Babuy); ارمنی is the ethnic adjective 'the Armenian'. گرد in the sequence is not serving one of its listed senses clearly in this context (it likely participates in a compound or epithet), so it cannot be matched to the provided options straightforwardly.
segments.s685-g158: که introduces the reported content 'that ...'. سالار ناپاک is 'the impure/unclean commander' (سالار 'commander, chief' + ناپاک 'impure'). کرد is the simple past 'did/made'. آن is the demonstrative 'that', and من (here 'me') is the affected person; the final ی is the archaic/copula-like ending seen in poetry (here indexing existence/state). The clause as a whole means 'that the impure commander had done that to me' (past reported action).
segments.s685-g159: کشیدند is third-person plural past of کشیدن. In this context it means 'they drew' (we choose the sense 'to draw (a weapon)'). شمشیر is the object 'sword'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and', and برخاستند is third-person plural past of برخاستن 'to rise, to stand up' — so: 'they drew swords and rose.'
segments.s685-g160: یکی ... دیگر ... is a distributive pattern 'one ... another ...'. یکی (here) = 'one (of them)', نو is the adjective 'new' modifying سخن 'speech/words'. آراستند is third-person plural past of آراستن 'to arrange/adorn/compose' — so the clause says 'one composed a new speech, another arranged (a different one)', with the plural verb reflecting actions by members of the group.
